pattern: Every value in [0, 1000] reachable from start is one BFS layer, and the moment any layer's frontier can jump straight to goal (even by leaving that range) gives the shortest operation count.
hints: Treat every integer from 0 to 1000 as a graph node with edges x -> x+v, x-v, x^v for each v in nums, and search outward from start layer by layer.
A move landing exactly on goal always ends the search immediately, even if goal itself is outside 0..1000 -- that branch is simply not allowed to continue afterward.
Bound the frontier to values that stay within 0..1000 (besides the goal-hitting moves), since only those states can spawn further operations; the first BFS layer that reaches goal is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: goal reached in a single addition
goal reached by jumping out of range in exactly one operation
parity is preserved by every available operation, goal is unreachable
must cross the entire 0..1000 range one step at a time
negative goal reached only by repeatedly subtracting past zero
several candidate operations at each step, xor included
start already close to goal but the only operations overshoot

stress.description: random distinct operands with a start/goal pair unlikely to be trivially reachable
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 200))
    nums = set()
    while len(nums) < m:
        nums.add(rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000))
    nums = list(nums)
    start = rng.randint(0, 1000)
    goal = rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000)
    while goal == start:
        goal = rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000)
    return [nums, start, goal]
stress.note: operand count capped so the O(1001 * m) BFS stays fast for both the primary solution and the full-completion oracle
